text: The surgeon creates four incisions between the eighth and ninth right intercostal spaces for the placement of robotic trocars.
The surgeon uses the da Vinci XI robotic system to perform a fissureless technique, approaching the hilum from anterior to posterior.

The surgeon incises the mediastinal pleura to expose the right upper pulmonary vein, which is the most anterior structure in the hilum. The vein is identified and isolated using blunt dissection and electrocautery, employing a fenestrated bipolar forceps in the left hand and a marrow gland in the right hand.
The surgeon carefully identifies and preserves the middle lobe vein, which typically drains into the right superior pulmonary vein, ensuring it remains intact during the procedure.
Following the complete isolation of the right upper pulmonary vein, the surgeon pulls the lobe inferiorly, providing clear visualization of the main pulmonary artery.
The surgeon isolates and removes a small hilar lymph node situated between the pulmonary artery and vein using robotic instruments.
The surgeon dissects the overlying pleura to reveal the pulmonary artery, located lateral to the superior vena cava, exposing both its upper and lower margins.
The surgeon uses a 30mm robotic vascular stapler to divide the right upper pulmonary vein, which has been previously isolated in the hilum.
The surgeon maneuvers the lobe to provide clear visualization of the dividing branches of the pulmonary artery, which are exposed after previous dissection steps.
The surgeon lifts the stump of the vein and dissects the adventitial plane of the pulmonary artery, exposing the Boyden branch running parallel to the pulmonary vein on its upper margin.
The surgeon performs lymphadenectomy at Heiler station X, facilitating the dissection of the Boyden branch on its lower margin, with visible manipulation of surrounding tissues.
The surgeon completes the isolation of the pulmonary artery and divides it using a 30mm robotic vascular stapler reload, ensuring precise separation of the artery from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon pulls the lung anteriorly and inferiorly to gain access to the bronchial structures, revealing the anatomical area for further dissection.
The surgeon dissects the posterior pleural reflection using electrocautery, revealing the right upper lobe bronchus beneath the pleura.
The surgeon performs gentle blunt dissection to free the upper margin of the Boyden branch from the lower margin of the azygos vein.
The surgeon clears lung tissue from the angle between the bronchus intermedius and the upper lobe bronchus, revealing the posterior hilar lymph nodes in the exposed area.
The surgeon pulls the lung lobe posteriorly, adjusting its position to facilitate further access to the bronchial structures.

The surgeon performs a hilar lymphadenectomy, allowing for the dissection of the lower margin of the bronchus. The bronchus is then transected using a robotic stapler equipped with a 45mm green reload.

The surgeon visually identifies the well-defined fissures between the upper, middle, and lower lobes of the lung. The division of these fissures is performed using multiple parenchymal reloads, separating the lobes with precision.

The surgeon begins a station seven lymphadenectomy by incising the posterior pleura from the superior edge of the lower lobe vein, following the course of the vagus nerve. This incision provides a clear view of the esophagus.

The surgeon approaches the paratracheal area by dissecting the mediastinal pleura above the azygos vein and along the lateral profile of the superior vena cava. Station IV-R and II-R lymph nodes are removed en bloc.
The surgeon completes the procedure by extracting the lobe from the chest using a specimen retrieval bag through a utility incision, which is created by joining the two middle robotic incisions.
